It's no small feat to scale a restaurant business to 400 locations in a decade; in fact, there are likely millions of great restaurants that have the cuisine and concept necessary for such scale, but they simply lack the will or competence to scale to that level.

Again, no small feat.

I also reduced CAVA's share count over the next ten years, implying that CAVA will begin a share buyback in the next 5 or so years, and I'm confident it will:

It has $366M in cash on its balance sheet and no debt. It generated free cash flow sustainably in 2024.

It will soon have ample cash which it won't need lying around. It doesn't need to empire build by buying adjacent businesses: its runway for growth is giant, especially when we think about international opportunities. Buying adjacent concepts would only serve to dilute focus and execution, harming shareholders in the process.

So I believe the 10% reduction in share count is prudent.

Lastly, I used a 15% free cash flow margin, which, to be sure, is well above CAVA's current free cash flow margin; however, because I know Chipotle so well, and because Chipotle's unit economics, identical as they are to CAVA's, have supported a 15% free cash flow margin, I believe this is acceptable to assume.

Turning to projected returns…

Total Projected Returns

I used a 35x exit multiple, which, I believe, is fair, though it might actually be conservative, considering the aforementioned runway for growth and CAVA's very high potential to sustain elevated growth well beyond 2033, similar to how Chipotle has grown at 15% far, far longer than I believe many anticipated it would.

And so 15% returns are attractive.

I would say, more than anything, this exercise just supports accumulating CAVA over time, as it dips during market panics, such as the one we're experiencing presently.

Summary: Risks And Buying Strategy

I'll say CAVA has been on a tear, though it is down from its lofty $150/share share price.

I'm certainly not jumping for joy at the opportunity we're being afforded here. I do like CAVA here, but I'm not over the moon about it due to its valuation. That is, this isn't a major market dislocation where market participants aren't seeing the value of CAVA: they get it. The market gets it, and the exceptional performance has been priced into the stock via an exceptional (high) valuation.

That said, my philosophy on CAVA has become:

As what will likely be considered a defining brand, akin to Chipotle or Netflix, I believe it makes sense to routinely accumulate the stock via a dollar-cost average strategy.

I've often thought, "I should just set up a DCA and blindly buy this every quarter for the next five years, then let it ride from there."

All of this said, there's of course risk. CAVA needs exceptional, pristine execution from management to grow at an aggressive 20%+ rate for 10 years straight. To expand internationally will be no small feat as well.

And, so, while I like the strategy I just shared above for those wanting to own CAVA, it's worth remembering that I often discuss growth stocks and growth stocks are risky, and the principal way to mitigate that risk is through healthy diversification.

In brief, I like CAVA and believe it will be higher in 5-10 years than where it currently trades.

data.title: Buy AMD Stock Now To Capitalize On The Generational AI Megatrend
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) stock, it has lost 6.4% in price, opening up a further buying opportunity.

AMD is one of the integral companies positioned at the nexus of a generational shift in economic history driven by AI. Global AI infrastructure spending is exploding, and the market is becoming more diverse. I envisage a Fiscal 2027 fair price for AMD stock of about $215, but I'm setting a take-profit of $200 because I think a severe overvaluation could occur well before this. Risk-wise, I find it incredibly important at the moment to manage a black swan portfolio. The Taiwan crisis risk is the primary factor that makes any portfolio a house of cards at present. Until the newly planned TSMC fabs are fully online in the USA, which may take years, equity investing is a high-risk pursuit which requires advanced hedging strategies.

Macro & AMD-Specific AI Growth Analysis

With hyperscalers investing unprecedented capital into AI infrastructure, a multi-decade growth trajectory for data center compute has become apparent to me. In aggregate, across Big Tech data center projects, nearly $400 billion will likely be spent on AI infrastructure globally in 2025. This is not a one-time spike. AMD's CEO Lisa Su has already noted that in data centers alone, the AI accelerator TAM is projected to grow at over a 60% CAGR to $500 billion in 2028. This is roughly the size of the entire semiconductor industry in 2023.

The bear case argues that AI investment might plateau after the initial rush. If companies find current AI models "good enough" or achieve efficiency gains, they might slow spending. DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) is one such company that has caused concerns from the market about smaller startups training competitive AI models cheaply, raising questions about the necessity of high AI capex. The consensus among Big Tech leaders appears to be to the contrary: increased efficiency will increase AI adoption. Essentially, the TAM will expand. This will include new major players like nation states. With AI becoming a utility, we're essentially seeing the foundation laid for what could be an indefinite build-out with long-term iterations into robotics and advanced autonomy.

AMD is well-positioned to capitalize on this trend, which should be the foundation for why anybody buys the stock at its current cheap valuation. Over the past 10 years, AMD has morphed itself into a provider of high-performance central and graphics processing units, as well as adaptive accelerators. AMD is one of the only companies capable of designing market-leading AI accelerators.

In 2023, AMD cemented its position as an integral AI infrastructure provider by launching its flagship Instinct MI300 series graphics processing units and accelerated processing units specifically aimed at AI training and inference in data centers. AMD's ROCm software stack, which acts as an open-source alternative to Nvidia's (NVDA) CUDA, is also foundational to its acting as integral linchpin of the AI revolution. ROCm is an example of the company lowering the barrier to adoption for AI, further expanding its TAM and increasing the penetration of AI technology across the globe with ease for consumers.

Following on from these strategic operational initiatives, AMD's data segment surged by 94% year-over-year in 2024 to $12.6 billion, primarily due to the ramp of Instinct MI300 AI accelerators and EPYC server CPUs. By Q4 2024, data center sales were up 69% year-over-year to a record $3.9 billion for the quarter; Instinct GPU shipments were a key driver.

Alex Karp, the CEO of Palantir (PLTR), one of the most powerful AI companies of the modern age, recently stated that the coming age "will be divided between AI haves and have-nots." It's worth encouraging the potential future have-nots to adopt AI now, because it appears essentially certain to me that this autonomous revolution will permeate and transform the global economy over the long term. Inequality of AI will likely be a real challenge of socioeconomics, but despite this concern, companies like AMD will very likely be at the epicenter of insatiable demand for status driven by AI efficiency and the associated monetary accrual. As a macro thesis, there is no other better industry to invest in right now; as a single-stock thesis, AMD is also attractively-valued and opportunistically-positioned.

Catalyst Analysis

Future growth catalysts for AMD include the full-scale ramp of MI300 series accelerators in 2025, shipping to multiple cloud providers and potentially enterprise customers and original equipment manufacturers. Later in 2025, AMD may introduce a successor; for example, MI350 series on 5nm/4nm with HBM3e. EPYC CPUs, such as the fifth generation "Turin," are also going to penetrate cloud and enterprise markets with increased performance and efficiency. Moreover, client and gaming innovations like the release of Ryzen processors with integrated AI capabilities and a refresh in the Radeon GPU lineup may stimulate demand for the segment.

Nations like India, Japan, France, and the United Arab Emirates are subsidizing domestic computing infrastructure and AI model development now, so we can expect AMD to be a key beneficiary of this trend. AMD PRO Technologies integrate enterprise-grade security and manageability features into processors, so this will be highly sought-after by sovereign (nation-state) AI developments. India aims to position its country as a global AI leader by 2030. Japan unveiled a $65 billion plan to support its domestic semiconductor and AI industries in 2024. France has announced a €109 billion investment plan in AI to bolster Europe's presence in the industry. AI is expected to contribute $96 billion to the UAE economy by 2030, accounting for 14% of its GDP.

Valuation & Price Targets

In my opinion, this is about the best time that one could invest in AMD stock. The price is below the 200-week moving average with an RSI value of ~35. That indicates extremely good value, and if you look back to July 2022, you will see the price hovered around the same dynamics before a strong bull run over the next 24 months. Given that AMD's near-term financial future is hinged on continued growth, the fundamental outlook is not one of decline, so the recent contraction is really sentiment-based and due for a substantial rebound over the next 24 months. Macro conditions, including a substantially lower interest rate environment likely to have occurred by and in FY26, will be highly contributory to such a rebound.

I'm targeting Fiscal 2027 revenue of $45 billion with a 30% net margin, equaling a net income of $13.5 billion. With diluted shares outstanding of 1.9 billion, this means a $7.11 EPS and an assumed terminal P/E ratio of 30. My stock price target is, therefore, $215. AMD is one of my largest positions, I have set a $200 take-profit as I think a significant overvaluation could occur ahead of time, potentially in H1 2026, and I would like to take profits ahead of fair value if this occurs. I will potentially re-buy the stock later if the valuation collapses again.

Risk Analysis

The long-term macro AI growth thesis is not without risks. The main of these pertains to a change in sentiment from AI hyperscalers and other customers around the utility of autonomy projects. ROI may have moments of weakness, but more importantly, a lack of clear vision and even moral hazards related to AI misuse at scale could cause serious clampdowns on the company's growth. I think AI operators have to be really clear on a well-defined set of values to penetrate through their AIs. The notion that AIs will be "value-independent" appears unbelievable to me. As such, the code that these systems are designed on needs to be fine-tuned with appropriate stewardship.

I consider the values at the foundation of the models to be the greatest threat to their long-term flourishing and acceptance by global markets. For example, if AIs running on AMD chips start performing autonomous actions which lead to the mass-scale firing of government or private-sector employees without full consideration of the socio-economic backdrop and consequences, AI could be largely deemed more dangerous than positive. The onus is on the AI programmers, fine-tuners, and architects at this fork in the road. Spirituality and value development seem like they will become more important than ever in the AI age.

Unfortunately, AMD's revenues will be directly caught in the crosshairs if AI misuse leads to severe regulatory crackdown and shutdown of AI. The threat of AI misuse will grow as AI agents proliferate, concentrating power in the wealthy who can afford the most powerful AI agents, making their values deeply penetrate into society for better or worse.

We should also starkly remember the major black-swan-style crisis which could occur in the next few years. I'm heavily invested in AI right now in my Nasdaq high-alpha black swan portfolio, but this is a house of cards. I'm 30% in cash, but an invasion or blockade of Taiwan by China's People's Liberation Army would lead to a drawdown of 50-90% of my portfolio value. I'd deploy my 30% cash position at the bottom into stocks I analyze as prudent at the time, largely negating the negative implications of the crisis on my portfolio. However, given that one simple blow can have such catastrophic effects on portfolios at this time, and the TSMC (TSM) plants outlined recently by the Trump administration are going to take years to be operational, I'm considering increasing my cash and bonds position to 40% for the time being.

These are perilous times amid a massive AI growth opportunity. Let's not forget the macro catalysts which could send our returns into a tailspin, and always prepare for the worst.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

AMD is a central player in what looks like a once-in-a-generation economic and technological shift toward AI. AI is not a fad—it's a fundamental driver of tech investments for the foreseeable future. Compute demand for AI is skyrocketing, and AMD has positioned itself as a primary provider. My Fiscal 2027 price target of $215 is conservative, and given the market's emotional nature, I'm betting a price of $200 will occur within the next 18 months. As a result, I've set a $200 take-profit point in my own holdings to capitalize on a potential overvaluation and trade in and out of the long-term thesis.

Remember though, these are perilous times geopolitically, and until America's manufacturing base is more secure, I'm holding 30% or more in cash because an invasion of Taiwan would devastate portfolios, especially tech-oriented ones like mine. The irony is that if you're prepared for the Taiwan crisis, you can come out with more money than if it never happened in the first place. I'll let you do the math on 40% of a portfolio in cash and bonds, liquidated after a 90% drop and invested in equities, appreciating back to what the equity values were pre-crisis. Let's hope it doesn't happen, but current conditions dictate prudence.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
